item.title: Jamie Dimon and his peers should beware of ending up on the UK ballot
item.description: There is a fine line between being ‘Concerned of Canary Wharf’ and becoming the subject for political discord yourself

item.title: How can Trump turn things around on inflation?
item.description: Most of the culpability rests with the Fed and the Biden government but voters will hold the president accountable

item.title: Tech titans should pick up the phone — and so should the rest of us
item.description: The lesson from Elon Musk’s case against OpenAI is that writing things down can be embarrassing

item.title: The century of the stalemate
item.description: In war, politics and other fields, it is ever harder to win

item.title: AI boom could end the de-equitisation ‘put’
item.description: This US bull market has not been accompanied by the usual deluge of equity issuance. Until now

item.title: Why doesn’t my credit score add up?
item.description: The bizarre system of credit scoring can punish the prudent and indebted alike

item.title: Greece has conquered white wine. Now it’s making exceptional rosé
item.description: The country’s wine revolution continues apace

item.title: Why the IPO of bike-sharing app Lime is no lemon
item.description: At a mooted $2bn enterprise value, Uber-backed company would trade at 28 times last year’s operating profit

item.title: Do you have a mango dealer?
item.description: You have to be in the know when sourcing the ‘king’ of these fruits

item.title: Colmado Wilmot: Brilliant, queue-free dining in Barcelona — restaurant review
item.description: An ex-El Bulli chef has gone back to basics, and the results are magical

item.title: Our dog-eat-dog world order needs a rethink
item.description: We must reinvent the governance of multilateral institutions

item.title: Xi, Trump and the Thucydides trap
item.description: A summit of low expectations did at least restore some stability

item.title: A cold peace between the US and China is good enough
item.description: Rather than trying to engineer a grand bargain, this is a moment to prevent deterioration

item.title: Party time for stock markets cannot last for ever
item.description: Investor mood is shifting to outright red-hot exuberance

item.title: QVC’s creditors have a nasty case of buyer’s remorse
item.description: Once a household name, the home-shopping TV network has filed for bankruptcy protection

item.title: Voters expect a prosperity our politicians cannot deliver
item.description: The markets will not allow the UK to borrow to maintain a standard of living that we have not earned

item.title: Labour’s faultlines are back with a vengeance
item.description: The past few weeks have exposed the cracks that Starmer has spent two years trying to paper over

item.title: Is the only way really down for stocks?
item.description: Market concentration means the majority of equities have room to rise

item.title: Is culture really as good for you as a workout?
item.description: As someone who has long practised culture over cross-training, I feel uniquely qualified to test this new proposition

item.title: The great global bond freakout
item.description: Plus, crypto’s not money

item.title: Why Warsh wants the Fed to keep quiet
item.description: The incoming chair thinks that constant ‘incantations’ do more harm than good

item.title: It could be time to bet on airlines — including unloved easyJet
item.description: Carrier’s enterprise valuation today is just four times operating profit, whereas in 2019 it was nine times

item.title: ‘Le Total bashing’ grips France over energy profits
item.description: Earnings jump since the Iran war has sparked heated calls for windfall taxes and criticism of returns of cash to shareholders

item.title: Treat the Chelsea Flower Show as a spectacle, not a shop window
item.description: Things have changed since 1963, but despite the departures there’s much on offer to relish

item.title: Emerging markets face Iran shocks with more debt, and less danger
item.description: Market reform has meant that large debts today are much less destabilising than smaller sums were in the past
